title: End-to-End Validation
description:
Integration and end-to-end tests that verify the full Vellum pipeline works — from user input through agent tool calls to compiled tablature output. Covers all three test pieces plus pipeline-level integration tests.

These tests use real LilyPond compilation, real music21 analysis, and real tonal.js calculations. LLM responses are the only thing mocked (to make tests deterministic), and even then we include a 'live agent' test that uses a real LLM call.

The goal: someone can clone the repo, run these tests, and verify every component works before touching the code.

title: BWV 996 Bourrée test
description:
End-to-end test using Bach BWV 996 Bourrée.

**What:**
Source the opening bars of BWV 996 Bourrée (public domain, widely available). Test as a purely instrumental baroque lute piece:

1. Compile .ly source on french-tab template → verify French tab output
2. check_playability() → verify difficulty assessment
3. Transpose to different key → verify diapason adjustment

**Why:** Simpler test than Flow My Tears (no voice part). Good smoke test for the instrumental pipeline.

**Test:**
- Compile → valid SVG
- Playability check returns reasonable difficulty (intermediate/advanced)
- Transpose up M2 → verify transposed output compiles

title: French tab pipeline test
description:
End-to-end test: generate LilyPond source for baroque lute French tab → compile → verify output.

**What:**
1. Programmatically create a LilyPond source using the french-tab.ly template with a D minor scale passage
2. Call POST /api/compile
3. Verify SVG output contains French letters (a, b, c, d)
4. Verify no compilation errors
5. Test with polyphonic voices (two voice layers)
6. Test with diapason bass notes
7. Verify MIDI output exists

**This is the critical pipeline test.** French tab is the primary output format for the primary instrument.

**Test (real LilyPond, no mocking):**
- Simple passage → SVG with letters
- Polyphonic → both voices render
- Diapasons → bass notes appear below staff
- Invalid source → errors returned, no SVG

title: Music21 analysis pipeline test
description:
End-to-end test: MusicXML → analyze → verify structured output.

**What:**
1. Create or source a simple SATB MusicXML file (a hymn tune or Bach chorale)
2. POST /api/analyze → verify key, Roman numerals, voice ranges
3. POST /api/chordify → verify chord-per-beat reduction
4. POST /api/lint on a passage with known parallel fifths → verify detection
5. POST /api/lint on a clean passage → verify no false positives

**Test (real music21, no mocking):**
- Analyze SATB → key is correct (e.g., C major)
- Chordify → first chord is identifiable (e.g., 'C major' or 'I')
- Lint with parallel fifths → violation detected
- Lint clean → no violations

title: Tool chain pipeline test
description:
Integration test: chain multiple tools together to simulate an arrangement workflow.

**What:**
1. Start with a melody (array of pitches)
2. tabulate() each pitch for baroque lute → get positions
3. voicings() for chord passages → get voicing options
4. check_playability() on the result → verify clean
5. Generate .ly source from the positions
6. compile() → verify SVG output

**This tests the data flow:** pitch → tabulate → position → voicing → playability → compile. No LLM needed — pure tool chain.

**Test (real tools + real LilyPond):**
- D minor scale → tabulate each note → positions on baroque lute → generate .ly → compile → SVG
- D minor chord → voicings → pick best → check_playability → no violations
- Add a deliberately bad voicing → check_playability catches it

title: Flow My Tears test scenarios
description:
End-to-end test using Dowland's Flow My Tears (Lachrimae).

**What:**
Source a public domain .ly or MusicXML of Flow My Tears (from Mutopia Project or hand-encode the opening bars). Test all 5 scenarios from SPEC.md:

1. Upload .ly source → compile → French tab + voice line (voice-and-tab template)
2. Intabulate vocal part for solo lute (merge voice into tab)
3. Convert to classical guitar (instrument swap)
4. Revoice a passage (modify a chord)
5. Change key (transpose + diapason retuning)

**Test approach:**
- Scenarios 1-2: Real LilyPond compilation, verify output
- Scenario 3: Use tabulate to remap, compile on guitar template
- Scenarios 4-5: Verify tools produce valid output
- LLM decisions can be simulated (hardcode the 'musical judgment' steps)

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- At least scenarios 1, 3, and 5 pass with real tool execution
- Output compiles to valid SVG
- No compilation errors in final output

**Test:**
- Compile Flow My Tears on french-tab template → SVG with letters
- Convert first 4 bars to classical guitar → SVG with numbers
- Transpose from A minor to D minor → retuned diapasons in output

title: BWV 996 Bourrée test
description:

**What:**

1. Compile .ly source on french-tab template → verify French tab output
2. check_playability() → verify difficulty assessment
3. Transpose to different key → verify diapason adjustment

**Why:** Simpler test than Flow My Tears (no voice part). Good smoke test for the instrumental pipeline.

title: All Creatures hymnal conversion test
description:
End-to-end test: SATB hymnal → baroque guitar arrangement.

**What:**
This is the most complex test — validates the full conversion pipeline from SPEC.md:

1. Source SATB MusicXML of 'All Creatures of Our God and King' (LASST UNS ERFREUEN, public domain 1623 tune)
2. analyze(musicxml) → key (D major), voices, Roman numeral progression
3. Use theory() to verify key, plan transposition to A minor
4. tabulate() pitches for baroque-guitar-5 → get positions
5. voicings() for chord passages (Alleluia refrains → rasgueado voicings)
6. check_playability() → verify clean
7. lint() the arrangement → verify no voice leading violations
8. compile() → produce tablature (number tab or French letter)

**This tests every layer:**
- Layer 1 (instrument mechanics): tabulate, voicings, check_playability
- Layer 2 (music theory): analyze, lint, theory
- LilyPond pipeline: compile

**Test approach:** LLM arrangement decisions are hardcoded (predetermined chord reductions, voice choices). Everything else is real.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- analyze() returns correct key (D major or nearby)
- Transposition to A minor produces valid pitch mapping
- At least one voicing per chord passage is playable
- lint() finds zero violations in the final arrangement (or only acceptable ones)
- Final output compiles to valid SVG

**Test:**
- Analyze hymn MusicXML → verify key
- Tabulate melody notes → all have valid positions on baroque guitar
- Compile final arrangement → valid SVG output

title: Agent conversation test (mocked LLM)
description:
Test the full agent loop: user message → LLM response → tool calls → compilation → result.

**What:**
A test that simulates a user conversation with a mocked LLM:

1. User sends: 'Arrange a D minor scale for baroque lute'
2. Mocked LLM responds with tool calls: diapasons('D minor'), then tabulate for each note, then generates .ly source, then calls compile
3. Verify each tool call executes correctly
4. Verify final compile produces valid SVG
5. Verify AgentToolResult content and details are correct

**Why mocked LLM:** LLM responses are non-deterministic. Mocking the LLM lets us test the tool execution pipeline deterministically. The mock returns predetermined tool call sequences that exercise the pipeline.

**What's NOT mocked:** Everything else — actual tool logic, actual LilyPond compilation, actual music21 analysis. The LLM is the only mock.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Agent processes the mocked conversation without errors
- All tool calls execute and return results
- Final compile result has SVG
- Tool renderers would receive correct details

**Test:**
- Run conversation → verify no errors thrown
- Verify compile was called and returned SVG
- Verify tool execution order matches expected sequence

title: Epic 12: NixOS Deployment
description:
Package Vellum as a Nix flake and NixOS module for deployment on servoid. The flake exports a package (buildNpmPackage for the Express server + Vite browser bundle) and a NixOS module that provides a systemd service, Traefik reverse proxy integration, and secrets management. Follows the same deployment pattern as existing services on servoid (A2A adapter, Hermes agent).

Domain: vellum.aoeu.pw
Server: servoid (NixOS, managed via jefffm/.nix flake)
Reverse proxy: Traefik with mTLS via step-ca

title: flake.nix package definition
description:
Define the Nix package for Vellum using buildNpmPackage.

**What:**
- flake.nix outputs.packages.default = buildNpmPackage that:
  1. Builds the Vite browser bundle (npx vite build → dist/)
  2. Bundles the Express server (TypeScript → JavaScript)
  3. Includes runtime dependencies: node_modules for server
  4. Wraps the entry point (node dist/server/index.js)
- Runtime dependencies (not build deps): lilypond, python3+music21
- Include instruments/ and templates/ directories in the package

**Considerations:**
- buildNpmPackage needs npmDepsHash — run `prefetch-npm-deps package-lock.json` to get it
- pi-web-ui has an xlsx dependency that fetches from a CDN URL — may need npmFlags or override for fetchNpmDeps (known issue from spec)
- LilyPond and Python+music21 are runtime deps passed via the NixOS module, not baked into the package

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `nix build` produces a working package
- Package includes dist/ (browser bundle), server code, instruments/, templates/
- Package runs: `./result/bin/vellum-server` starts the Express server

**Test:**
- `nix build` exits 0
- `./result/bin/vellum-server --help` or health check works
- Verify dist/index.html exists in the output

title: NixOS module
description:
Create the NixOS module that the jefffm/.nix flake consumes.

**What:**
The flake exports nixosModules.default providing:

```nix
options.services.vellum = {
  enable = mkEnableOption;
  port = mkOption { type = types.port; default = 3000; };
  domain = mkOption { type = types.str; default = "vellum.aoeu.pw"; };
  apiKeyFile = mkOption { type = types.path; };  # sops-nix or agenix
  dataDir = mkOption { type = types.path; default = "/var/lib/vellum"; };
};
```

config provides:
- systemd.services.vellum — runs the Express server
  - Environment: PORT, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (from apiKeyFile), ExecStart: ${package}/bin/vellum-server
  - DynamicUser or dedicated user
  - StateDirectory = "vellum" (for arrangements/)
  - PATH includes lilypond and python3+music21
- services.traefik.dynamicConfigOptions for vellum.aoeu.pw
  - mTLS via step-ca (existing servoid pattern)

**Considerations:**
- LilyPond and Python+music21 must be in the service's PATH
- API key loaded from file at runtime (not in nix store)
- Data directory writable for arrangement persistence
- Follow existing servoid service patterns (check jefffm/.nix for examples)

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- `services.vellum.enable = true` in servoid config → service starts
- systemd service runs and passes health check
- Traefik routes vellum.aoeu.pw to the service
- API key available to the process
- LilyPond and Python accessible from the service

**Test:**
- Deploy to servoid → `curl https://vellum.aoeu.pw/health` returns 200
- `systemctl status vellum` shows active
- POST /api/compile with a simple .ly → returns SVG (proves LilyPond is in PATH)
- POST /api/analyze with simple MusicXML → returns analysis (proves music21 is available)

title: Deployment smoke test
description:
Post-deployment verification that the full stack works on servoid.

**What:**
A shell script or test suite that verifies the production deployment:

1. Health check: GET /health → 200
2. LLM proxy: POST /api/stream with a simple prompt → streamed response (requires API key)
3. LilyPond: POST /api/compile with simple .ly → SVG returned
4. Music21: POST /api/analyze with simple MusicXML → analysis returned
5. Instruments: GET /api/instruments → list of profiles
6. Templates: GET /api/templates/french-tab → template source
7. Frontend: GET / → HTML with chat-panel
8. mTLS: verify certificate is valid for vellum.aoeu.pw

**Why:** Deployment can fail silently — wrong PATH, missing deps, port conflicts. This smoke test catches issues immediately after deploy.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- All 8 checks pass
- Script can be run from any machine with curl + mTLS cert

**Test:**
- Run script against production → all green

title: Browser Theory & Compile Tools
description:
Browser-side AgentTool<T> wrappers for tools that call server endpoints (compile, analyze, lint) and the browser-native theory tool (tonal.js). These are thin wrappers: they define the TypeBox parameter schema, make a fetch() call to the server API, and return an AgentToolResult with content (text for LLM) and details (structured data for UI renderers).

The theory tool is the exception — it runs entirely in the browser via tonal.js with no server round-trip.

title: compile tool — server fetch wrapper
description:
Implement the compile AgentTool<T> that calls POST /api/compile.

**What:**
- Input: { source: string, format?: 'svg' | 'pdf' | 'both' }
- fetch() to /api/compile with JSON body
- Returns AgentToolResult: content (text summary for LLM), details (CompileResult with SVG/PDF/MIDI for UI renderer)
- On success: 'Compiled successfully. N bars, M voices. No errors.'
- On failure: 'Compilation failed with N error(s): [structured error list]'
- Respects AbortSignal for cancellation

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Valid .ly source → returns SVG in details.svg
- Invalid source → returns errors in both content and details
- AbortSignal cancellation works

**Test:**
- Compile simple score → verify content says 'Compiled successfully'
- Compile invalid → verify content says 'failed' with error count
- Verify details.svg is non-empty on success

title: analyze tool — server fetch wrapper
description:
Implement the analyze AgentTool<T> that calls POST /api/analyze.

**What:**
- Input: { source: string, format?: 'musicxml' | 'lilypond' }
- fetch() to /api/analyze with JSON body
- Returns AgentToolResult: content (formatted text analysis for LLM), details (AnalysisResult)

**Content format for LLM:**
```
Key: D major (confidence: high)
Time: 4/4
Voices: Soprano (D4–E5), Alto (A3–B4), Tenor (D3–A3), Bass (G2–D3)
Chord progression:
  Bar 1: I | V6 | vi | IV
  Bar 2: ii | V7 | I | I
```

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- MusicXML input → returns key, chords, voices in content
- details contains structured AnalysisResult
- Error from server → content describes the error

**Test:**
- Analyze SATB MusicXML → verify content includes key name
- Verify details.key, details.voices, details.chords are populated

title: lint tool — server fetch wrapper
description:
Implement the lint AgentTool<T> that calls POST /api/lint.

**What:**
- Input: { source: string, format?: 'musicxml' | 'lilypond', rules?: string[] }
- fetch() to /api/lint with JSON body
- Returns AgentToolResult: content (formatted violation list for LLM), details (violations array)

**Content format for LLM:**
```
3 violations found:
  Bar 4, beat 1: Parallel fifths between soprano and bass
  Bar 7, beat 3: Voice crossing — alto above soprano
  Bar 12, beat 1: Unresolved leading tone in tenor
```
Or: 'No voice leading violations found. Passage is clean.'

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Passage with violations → content lists them
- Clean passage → content says 'no violations'
- details.violations array matches content

**Test:**
- Lint passage with known parallel fifths → verify in content
- Lint clean passage → verify 'no violations'

title: Epic: Foundation & Dev Environment
description:
Project scaffolding, Nix dev shell, and dependency setup for Vellum. This is the first thing that must be done — everything else depends on a working project structure, build pipeline, and dev environment with LilyPond + music21 + Node.js available.

## Deliverables
- package.json with all dependencies (pi-agent-core, pi-web-ui, pi-ai, tonal, @sinclair/typebox)
- tsconfig.json for TypeScript compilation
- vite.config.ts for browser bundle
- flake.nix with dev shell providing LilyPond 2.24.x, Python3+music21, Node.js 20+
- Directory structure matching SPEC.md: src/, src/server/, instruments/, templates/, arrangements/, sources/, public/
- Basic index.html shell in public/

## Acceptance Criteria
- `nix develop` drops into a shell with lilypond, python3 (with music21 importable), node, npm all available
- `npm install` succeeds
- `npx vite build` produces a browser bundle (even if empty)
- `npx tsc --noEmit` passes
- Directory structure exists as specified

## Tests
- `lilypond --version` shows 2.24.x in the nix shell
- `python3 -c 'import music21; print(music21.VERSION_STR)'` succeeds
- `node --version` shows v20+
- `npx vite build` exits 0
- `npx tsc --noEmit` exits 0

title: Nix dev shell with LilyPond, music21, Node.js
description:
Create flake.nix providing a development shell with all required system dependencies.

## What
flake.nix with:
- devShell providing: nodejs_20, lilypond (2.24.x), python3 with music21, npm
- The package definition (buildNpmPackage) can be a stub for now — full packaging is in the deployment epic
- inputs: nixpkgs (unstable or 24.05+)

## Why
LilyPond and music21 are system-level dependencies that can't be installed via npm. Nix ensures reproducible dev environments. The flake also eventually provides the NixOS module for production deployment.

## How
```nix
devShells.default = pkgs.mkShell {
  buildInputs = [
    pkgs.nodejs_20
    pkgs.lilypond
    (pkgs.python3.withPackages (ps: [ ps.music21 ]))
  ];
};
```

## Considerations
- LilyPond 2.24.4 is in nixpkgs unstable. Verify it's available in the chosen nixpkgs revision.
- music21 may pull in matplotlib, numpy, etc. as transitive deps — that's fine, Nix handles it.
- The xlsx CDN tarball URL in pi-web-ui's dependency tree may need lockfile patching for fetchNpmDeps in the eventual buildNpmPackage. Note this for the deployment epic but don't block on it here.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `nix develop` enters a shell
- `lilypond --version` shows 2.24.x
- `python3 -c 'import music21'` succeeds
- `node --version` shows v20+
- `npm --version` works

## Test
Run each version check command above. All must succeed.

title: Epic: Music21 Theory Engine
description:
Server-side music theory analysis engine using Python's music21 library. Provides MusicXML parsing, harmonic analysis (key detection, Roman numerals, chord-per-beat reduction), and voice leading rule checking (parallel fifths/octaves, voice crossing, spacing). Called as a Python subprocess from Express endpoints — same deployment pattern as LilyPond.

The theory engine fills the gap between instrument mechanics tools (Layer 1 — 'can this be played?') and the LLM's musical judgment (Layer 3 — 'does this sound good?'). Layer 2 answers: 'is this correct music theory?'

Implementation: src/server/theory.py is a CLI script with subcommands (analyze, lint, chordify). Express endpoints in src/server/theory.ts spawn it as a subprocess, pass input via stdin, read JSON from stdout.

title: theory.py — chordify subcommand
description:
Implement the 'chordify' subcommand for chord-per-beat harmonic reduction.

**What:**
`python3 theory.py chordify` reads MusicXML from stdin, returns chord-per-beat analysis:
{
  chords: [
    { bar: 1, beat: 1, pitches: ['D4', 'F#4', 'A4', 'D5'], name: 'D major', duration: 1.0 },
    { bar: 1, beat: 2, pitches: ['A3', 'E4', 'A4', 'C#5'], name: 'A major', duration: 1.0 },
    ...
  ]
}

**music21 operations:**
- converter.parse() → Score
- score.chordify() → chord reduction
- For each chord: extract pitches, identify chord name, get beat position

**Why:** This is how the hymnal conversion workflow gets from SATB voices to a chord chart the LLM can use for arrangement decisions. chordify() collapses multiple voices into a single chord stream.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Reduces 4-voice SATB to chord-per-beat
- Each chord includes pitches, chord name, bar/beat position
- Handles non-chord tones (passing tones, suspensions) by taking the vertical sonority

**Test:**
- Chordify a simple SATB passage → verify chord names match expected
- Chordify a passage with suspensions → verify chords are captured at beat positions

title: Server endpoints for theory engine (/api/analyze, /api/lint, /api/chordify)
description:
Integration tests for the music21 theory engine using real MusicXML scores.

**What:**
A test suite that verifies the entire music21 pipeline with real music:

1. **Key detection test:** Analyze a Bach chorale (available in music21 corpus) → verify correct key
2. **Roman numeral test:** Analyze a known I-IV-V-I progression → verify Roman numerals
3. **Voice range test:** Analyze SATB score → verify soprano/alto/tenor/bass ranges match expected
4. **Lint test — parallel fifths:** Create a passage with deliberate parallel fifths → verify lint catches them
5. **Lint test — clean passage:** Analyze a well-written chorale → verify no or minimal violations
6. **Chordify test:** Reduce a known SATB passage → verify chord names
7. **Error handling test:** Send garbage input → verify graceful error
8. **Hymnal test prep:** If possible, test with a simple hymn tune (All Creatures) MusicXML

**Why:** The music21 engine is the foundation of the hymnal conversion workflow. These tests prove it works with real music, not toy examples.

**Test approach:** Real music21 — no mocking. Uses music21's built-in corpus (Bach chorales) plus hand-crafted test MusicXML. Tests require the nix shell (Python + music21 installed).

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- All tests pass with real music21
- Key detection correct for at least major/minor keys
- Lint detects known voice leading errors
- Clean passages have zero false positives (or very few)
- Tests run in < 60 seconds

title: Music21 integration test suite
description:
Implement the 'analyze' subcommand in src/server/theory.py.

**What:**
`python3 theory.py analyze` reads MusicXML from stdin, outputs JSON to stdout:
{
  key: 'D major',
  timeSignature: '4/4',
  voices: [{ name: 'Soprano', lowest: 'D4', highest: 'E5' }, ...],
  chords: [{ bar: 1, beat: 1, roman: 'I', name: 'D major', notes: ['D', 'F#', 'A'] }, ...]
}

**music21 operations:**
1. converter.parse(stdin) → Score object
2. score.analyze('key') → Key (Krumhansl-Schmuckler algorithm)
3. score.chordify() → chordified score
4. For each chord: roman.romanNumeralFromChord(chord, key) → Roman numeral
5. For each part: extract range (lowest/highest pitch)
6. Get time signature from first measure

**Why:** This is the critical first step for any conversion workflow (hymnal → guitar, piano → lute). The LLM needs to know what key the piece is in, what chords it uses, and what voices are present before it can make arrangement decisions.

**How:**
```python
import sys, json
from music21 import converter, roman, key, stream

def analyze():
    score = converter.parse(sys.stdin.read())
    analyzed_key = score.analyze('key')
    chordified = score.chordify()
    # ... extract Roman numerals, voice ranges, time sig
    print(json.dumps(result))
```

**Considerations:**
- music21's analyze('key') can return incorrect results for short excerpts or modal music. Include confidence score.
- chordify() may produce overly dense results — one chord per unique vertical sonority. May want to filter to chord changes only.
- MusicXML from different sources (MuseScore, Finale, hymnary.org) may have different quality. Test with multiple sources.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Accepts MusicXML via stdin, produces valid JSON on stdout
- Key detection works for major/minor keys
- Roman numeral analysis produces correct results for simple progressions
- Voice ranges correctly extracted
- Handles at least 4-voice SATB scores

**Test (integration — real music21, no mocking):**
- Analyze a simple SATB chorale in C major → verify key='C major', chords include I, IV, V
- Analyze a hymn tune in D major → verify key detection
- Analyze a score with known chord progression → verify Roman numerals match
- Test with malformed MusicXML → verify graceful error

2. score.analyze('key') → Key (Krumhansl-Schmuckler algorithm)
3. score.chordify() → chordified score
4. For each chord: roman.romanNumeralFromChord(chord, key) → Roman numeral
5. For each part: extract range (lowest/highest pitch)
6. Get time signature from first measure

**Why:** This is the critical first step for any conversion workflow (hymnal → guitar, piano → lute). The LLM needs to know what key the piece is in, what chords it uses, and what voices are present before it can make arrangement decisions.

**How:**
```python
import sys, json
from music21 import converter, roman, key, stream

def analyze():
    score = converter.parse(sys.stdin.read())
    analyzed_key = score.analyze('key')
    chordified = score.chordify()
    # ... extract Roman numerals, voice ranges, time sig
    print(json.dumps(result))
```

**Considerations:**
- music21's analyze('key') can return incorrect results for short excerpts or modal music. Include confidence score.
- chordify() may produce overly dense results — one chord per unique vertical sonority. May want to filter to chord changes only.
- MusicXML from different sources (MuseScore, Finale, hymnary.org) may have different quality. Test with multiple sources.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Accepts MusicXML via stdin, produces valid JSON on stdout
- Key detection works for major/minor keys
- Roman numeral analysis produces correct results for simple progressions
- Voice ranges correctly extracted
- Handles at least 4-voice SATB scores

**Test (integration — real music21, no mocking):**
- Analyze a simple SATB chorale in C major → verify key='C major', chords include I, IV, V
- Analyze a hymn tune in D major → verify key detection
- Analyze a score with known chord progression → verify Roman numerals match
- Test with malformed MusicXML → verify graceful error

title: theory.py — lint subcommand
description:
Implement the 'lint' subcommand for voice leading rule checking.

**What:**
`python3 theory.py lint` reads a passage (MusicXML or LilyPond) from stdin, checks voice leading rules, outputs JSON:
{
  violations: [
    { bar: 4, beat: 1, type: 'parallel_fifths', description: 'Parallel fifths between soprano (A4→B4) and bass (D3→E3)', voices: ['soprano', 'bass'] },
    ...
  ]
}

**Rules checked:**
- parallel_fifths: two voices moving in parallel perfect fifths
- parallel_octaves: two voices moving in parallel octaves
- voice_crossing: a lower voice goes above an upper voice
- spacing: voices too far apart (> P8 between adjacent upper voices)
- direct_octaves: similar motion to a perfect octave/fifth (hidden parallels)
- unresolved_leading_tone: leading tone (scale degree 7) doesn't resolve up

**music21 operations:**
- Parse score → extract voice pairs
- voiceLeading.VoiceLeadingQuartet for each pair of adjacent voices
- Check parallelMotion(), antiParallelMotion(), etc.
- Check voice crossing by comparing pitch registers

**Why:** The LLM generates arrangements but can't reliably self-check for voice leading violations. This tool catches mechanical errors deterministically.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Detects parallel fifths in a known passage
- Detects voice crossing
- Returns empty violations for a correctly written passage
- Each violation has bar, beat, type, description, and involved voices

**Test (integration — real music21):**
- Create a 4-voice passage with deliberate parallel fifths → verify detected
- Create a passage with voice crossing → verify detected
- Create a clean Bach chorale → verify zero or minimal violations
- Test with 2-voice passage → verify works (not just 4-voice)

title: theory.py — chordify subcommand (chord-per-beat reduction)
description:
Implement Express endpoints that call theory.py as a subprocess.

**What:**
- POST /api/analyze — accepts { source: string, format?: 'musicxml' | 'lilypond' }, spawns `python3 theory.py analyze`, passes source via stdin, returns JSON stdout
- POST /api/lint — accepts { source: string, format?: 'musicxml' | 'lilypond', rules?: string[] }, spawns `python3 theory.py lint`, returns violations
- POST /api/chordify — accepts { source: string }, spawns `python3 theory.py chordify`, returns chords

**How:**
- Use child_process.execFile or spawn
- Pipe the source string to stdin
- Read stdout as JSON, stderr as error details
- Timeout: 30 seconds
- Handle Python errors (non-zero exit, stderr output) → return 500 with error message

**Considerations:**
- Python path: use `python3` (in PATH via nix)
- theory.py path: resolve relative to server directory
- Input format: MusicXML is primary, LilyPond support may need conversion (v2)
- Large scores: stdin pipe handles any size, but parsing time grows

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- POST /api/analyze with MusicXML → returns analysis JSON
- POST /api/lint with a passage → returns violations JSON
- POST /api/chordify with MusicXML → returns chords JSON
- Python errors → 500 with structured error message
- Timeout enforced

**Test:**
- POST /api/analyze with simple SATB MusicXML → verify key and chords in response
- POST /api/lint with known parallel fifths → verify violation detected
- POST with malformed input → verify 500 with error
- POST with empty body → verify 400

title: Epic 5: Express Server Core
description:
Express server that serves static browser assets, proxies LLM API calls, and provides REST endpoints for tools. Runs on servoid (NixOS). This is the backbone — compile, analyze, lint, instruments, templates, and arrangements all hang off this server.

Server code lives in src/server/. Entry point: src/server/index.ts.

Endpoints (full list):
- POST /api/stream — LLM proxy via pi-agent-core streamProxy
- POST /api/compile — LilyPond subprocess
- POST /api/validate — LilyPond syntax check
- GET /api/instruments — list profiles
- GET /api/instruments/:id — single profile
- GET /api/templates/:name — template source
- GET /api/arrangements — list saved
- POST /api/arrangements — save
- GET /api/arrangements/:id — retrieve
- POST /api/analyze — music21 analysis
- POST /api/lint — music21 voice leading
- POST /api/chordify — music21 chord reduction

The server also serves the Vite-built browser bundle from dist/ as static files.

title: Express server skeleton
description:
Create src/server/index.ts — Express server with middleware, static asset serving, health check.

**What:**
- Express app with JSON body parser, CORS (dev mode), error handling middleware
- Serve static files from dist/ (Vite output) for production
- GET /health endpoint returning { status: 'ok', version: ... }
- Port configurable via PORT env var (default 3000)
- Graceful shutdown on SIGTERM/SIGINT
- Route mounting point for all /api/* routes (stub routers imported from other modules)

**Why:** Everything hangs off this server. Must be rock-solid and simple.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Server starts on configured port
- GET /health returns 200 with JSON status
- Static files served from dist/
- Graceful shutdown works
- Error middleware catches and formats errors as JSON

**Test:**
- Start server → GET /health → expect 200 { status: 'ok' }
- Start server → GET /nonexistent → expect 404
- Start server → send SIGTERM → expect clean exit

title: LLM proxy via streamProxy
description:
Implement POST /api/stream using pi-agent-core's streamProxy utility.

**What:**
- POST /api/stream endpoint that proxies LLM API calls to the configured provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.)
- API key loaded from environment variable or file (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, or path from apiKeyFile)
- Uses streamProxy from @mariozechner/pi-agent-core — this handles the SSE streaming protocol
- Never exposes the API key to the browser

**Why:** The Agent runs in the browser but can't hold API keys. streamProxy is the pi-mono pattern for this — the browser Agent sends requests to /api/stream, the server adds the API key and forwards to the provider.

**How:** Import streamProxy from pi-agent-core, mount it as Express middleware on POST /api/stream. Pass provider config (API key, model, etc.) from server-side environment.

**Considerations:**
- Need to understand streamProxy's exact API — read pi-agent-core source/docs
- May need to configure which provider (Anthropic vs OpenAI) via env var
- Streaming responses must be forwarded correctly (SSE or chunked transfer)

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- POST /api/stream with valid request body proxies to LLM provider
- API key is added server-side, never sent to browser
- Streaming responses reach the browser client
- Invalid requests return structured error

**Test:**
- Start server with valid API key → send a simple prompt via curl → get streamed response
- Start server without API key → POST /api/stream → get 500 with clear error message
- Verify API key does not appear in response headers or body

title: GET /api/instruments endpoints
description:
Implement instrument profile serving endpoints.

**What:**
- GET /api/instruments — returns JSON array of all instrument profile summaries (id, name, type, courses/strings count)
- GET /api/instruments/:id — returns full YAML-parsed instrument profile as JSON
- Uses the profile loader from the instruments epic

**Why:** Browser loads profiles at init for system prompt injection. Tools may also fetch profiles for runtime decisions.

**How:** Import loadAllProfiles() and loadProfile(id) from the profile loader module. Wrap in Express route handlers.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- GET /api/instruments returns all profiles (7+ entries)
- GET /api/instruments/baroque-lute-13 returns the full baroque lute profile
- GET /api/instruments/nonexistent returns 404
- Response Content-Type is application/json

**Test:**
- GET /api/instruments → verify array length >= 7
- GET /api/instruments/baroque-lute-13 → verify id, tuning array length = 13
- GET /api/instruments/fake → 404

title: GET /api/templates endpoint
description:
Implement template serving endpoint.

**What:**
- GET /api/templates/:name — returns the raw LilyPond template source as text
- Templates served from templates/ directory
- Content-Type: text/plain (it's LilyPond source code)

**Why:** The LLM agent may need to read template source to understand the structure before generating music content that fits into it. Also useful for debugging.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- GET /api/templates/french-tab returns the french-tab.ly content
- GET /api/templates/nonexistent returns 404
- Response is plain text

**Test:**
- GET /api/templates/french-tab → response contains '\version' and 'fret-letter-tablature-format'
- GET /api/templates/fake → 404

title: GET/POST /api/arrangements endpoints
description:
Implement arrangement persistence endpoints.

**What:**
- GET /api/arrangements — list saved arrangements (id, title, instrument, created_at)
- POST /api/arrangements — save an arrangement { title, instrument, lySource, metadata }
- GET /api/arrangements/:id — retrieve a saved arrangement
- Storage: JSON files in arrangements/ directory on server (simple file-based for v1)
- Each arrangement gets a UUID and timestamp

**Why:** Users need to save and retrieve arrangements across sessions. For v1, simple file-based storage is sufficient. Server-side persistence with proper session management is v2.

**Considerations:**
- v1 storage is file-based (JSON files in arrangements/ dir). No database.
- Should store the .ly source, instrument profile ID, and metadata (key, title, etc.)
- UUID generation for arrangement IDs

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- POST with valid body → arrangement saved, returns { id, title }
- GET /api/arrangements → lists all saved arrangements
- GET /api/arrangements/:id → returns the full arrangement including .ly source
- Persistence survives server restart (file-based)

**Test:**
- POST an arrangement → GET it back → verify content matches
- POST two arrangements → GET list → verify both appear
- GET /api/arrangements/nonexistent → 404

title: Epic 6: LilyPond Compilation Service
description:
Server-side LilyPond compilation endpoints. Takes .ly source code, runs LilyPond as a subprocess, returns SVG/PDF/MIDI artifacts and structured errors. This is the core rendering pipeline — every arrangement the agent produces must go through compile to become viewable tablature.

The critical innovation: LilyPond stderr is parsed into structured CompileError objects (bar number, beat, type, message) instead of dumping raw Guile stack traces on the LLM. This makes the error → fix → recompile loop reliable.

title: Task 6.1: POST /api/compile — LilyPond subprocess management
description:
Implement the compile endpoint that runs LilyPond as a subprocess.

**What:**
- POST /api/compile accepts { source: string, format?: 'svg' | 'pdf' | 'both' }
- Writes source to a temp file in a unique temp directory
- Spawns lilypond subprocess with appropriate flags:
  - SVG: `lilypond --svg -o output source.ly`
  - PDF: `lilypond -o output source.ly`
  - Both: run once, LilyPond produces both when configured
- Reads output files (SVG as text, PDF/MIDI as base64)
- Cleans up temp directory after response
- Returns { svg?: string, pdf?: string, midi?: string, errors: CompileError[], barCount?: number, voiceCount?: number }
- Timeout: 30 seconds for compilation (LilyPond should be fast)
- On compilation failure: still parse errors, return empty artifacts

**Why:** This is the rendering pipeline. Every arrangement must compile to be useful. LilyPond is the only tool that can produce publication-quality tablature.

**How:**
- Use child_process.execFile or spawn to run lilypond
- Use a unique tmpdir per compilation (avoid collisions from concurrent requests)
- Read stdout/stderr, parse errors (see task 6.2)
- Clean up tmpdir in finally block

**Considerations:**
- LilyPond path: use `lilypond` (in PATH via nix). Do not hardcode paths.
- Concurrent compilations: each gets its own tmpdir, no shared state
- Large outputs: SVG can be big for multi-page scores. Stream if needed (v2).
- MIDI: LilyPond only produces MIDI if the .ly has \midi { } block

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- POST with valid .ly source → returns SVG string in response
- POST with invalid .ly → returns errors array with structured error objects
- POST with format='pdf' → returns base64-encoded PDF
- Compilation respects timeout (no hanging on pathological input)
- Temp files are cleaned up even on error

**Test (integration — real LilyPond):**
- Compile a simple score: `\version "2.24.0" { c'4 d' e' f' }` → verify SVG contains note elements
- Compile invalid source: `{ invalid !!! }` → verify errors array is non-empty with structured messages
- Compile a template with sample music → verify SVG is non-empty
- Compile two requests concurrently → both succeed (no temp file collision)

title: Task 6.2: LilyPond stderr → structured error parsing
description:
Parse LilyPond's stderr output into structured CompileError objects.

**What:**
A function parseLilyPondErrors(stderr: string, source: string): CompileError[] that:
- Extracts error messages from LilyPond stderr
- Maps line numbers to bar numbers (by counting bar checks '|' and \bar in the source)
- Categorizes error types: syntax, note_out_of_range, undefined_variable, etc.
- Handles multi-line error messages
- Ignores warnings (unless they indicate real problems)

**LilyPond stderr format:**
```
source.ly:15:5: error: syntax error, unexpected STRING
  d'4 badtoken
      ^
source.ly:22:1: warning: barcheck failed at: 3/4
```

Pattern: `filename:line:col: (error|warning): message`

**Why:** Raw LilyPond errors are Guile stack traces mixed with musical diagnostics. Structured errors let the LLM understand what failed and fix it intelligently. 'Bar 8: syntax error' is actionable. '(ice-9/boot-9.scm:1738:1): ...' is not.

**How:**
- Regex pattern: `/^(.+?):(\d+):(\d+):\s*(error|warning):\s*(.+)$/gm`
- Line → bar mapping: scan the source for bar checks (|), measure numbers, time signatures
- Error classification based on message content

**Considerations:**
- Bar number mapping is approximate — LilyPond does not always report bar numbers directly
- Some errors span multiple lines in stderr — need to handle continuation lines
- Warnings about barcheck failures are actually useful (indicate rhythm errors)

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Parses standard LilyPond error format into CompileError[]
- Each CompileError has: bar (best effort), beat (if available), line, type, message
- Handles at least: syntax errors, undefined variables, barcheck failures
- Returns empty array for clean compilation

**Test:**
- Parse sample stderr with known error → verify structured output
- Parse stderr with multiple errors → verify all are captured
- Parse clean stderr (warnings only) → verify empty or warning-only array

title: Task 6.3: POST /api/validate — syntax-only check
description:
Lightweight syntax validation endpoint.

**What:**
- POST /api/validate accepts { source: string }
- Runs LilyPond with `--loglevel=ERROR` and dry-run flags
- Returns { valid: boolean, errors: CompileError[] }
- Does not produce output files (faster than full compile)

**Why:** Quick syntax check before full compilation. Useful during iterative editing — the agent can validate source before committing to a full compile cycle.

**How:** Similar to /api/compile but with flags that skip rendering. LilyPond's `-dno-print-pages` or similar flag. Stderr parsing reuses parseLilyPondErrors().

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Valid source → { valid: true, errors: [] }
- Invalid source → { valid: false, errors: [...] }
- Faster than full compile (no SVG/PDF generation)

**Test:**
- Validate valid source → expect valid: true
- Validate invalid source → expect valid: false with errors
- Time validate vs compile → validate should be faster

title: Task 6.4: Integration test: compile French tab template
description:
End-to-end test: fill the french-tab.ly template with real music, compile, verify output.

**What:**
A test that:
1. Loads the french-tab.ly template
2. Inserts a real musical passage (e.g., a simple D minor scale on baroque lute)
3. Sends to POST /api/compile
4. Verifies:
   - SVG output is returned
   - SVG contains French letters (a, b, c, d) not numbers
   - SVG shows tab staff lines
   - MIDI file is produced
   - No compilation errors

**Why:** This is the critical integration test proving the entire LilyPond pipeline works for the primary output format. If French tab does not compile correctly, the whole project fails.

**Test (real LilyPond, no mocking):**
- Compile D minor scale on baroque lute → SVG with French letters
- Compile a chord with diapason bass note → verify diapason notation in SVG
- Compile polyphonic passage (two voices) → verify both render
- Check MIDI output contains expected number of notes

title: Instrument Mechanics Tools
description:
Browser-side AgentTool<T> implementations for instrument-specific mechanical correctness: pitch-to-position mapping, chord voicing enumeration, playability validation, transposition, diapason tuning, and fretboard diagrams. These are Layer 1 tools — they answer 'can this be played on this instrument?'

All tools use instrument profiles loaded at init. For v1, they compute locally in the browser using cached profile data (no server round-trip needed for pure computation). Each tool follows the AgentTool<T> pattern from pi-agent-core with TypeBox parameter schemas and dual-channel AgentToolResult (content for LLM, details for UI).

These tools are what differentiate Vellum from a generic LLM with bash access. The LLM should never guess fret positions or chord voicings — it should call these tools.

title: tabulate tool — pitch → position lookup
description:
Implement the tabulate AgentTool<T> that maps a pitch to all valid course/fret positions on a given instrument.

**What:**
- Input: { pitch: string, instrument: string } (e.g., 'F4', 'baroque-lute-13')
- For each course on the instrument, calculate which fret (if any) produces the target pitch
- Return all valid positions ranked by quality: open string > low fret (1-3) > high fret (4-8)
- For diapason courses: only return if the pitch exactly matches the open tuning
- For re-entrant courses (baroque guitar): account for the octave displacement

**Algorithm:**
1. Load instrument profile → tuning array
2. For each course, the open pitch + N frets = pitch at fret N
3. If target pitch matches any fret 0-maxFrets: add TabPosition
4. For diapasons: fret must be 0 (open only)
5. For re-entrant: use the actual sounding pitch (may be octave higher)
6. Rank: open > frets 1-3 > frets 4-8

**Pitch math:** Each fret = 1 semitone. Use MIDI note numbers for comparison (A4=69, each semitone = +1). Convert note name → MIDI number, compare.

**Returns to LLM (content):** 'F4 on baroque-lute-13: course 1 fret 0 (open, preferred), course 2 fret 3 (low fret)'
**Returns to UI (details):** { positions: TabPosition[] }

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- tabulate('F4', 'baroque-lute-13') returns course 1 fret 0 (open, quality='open')
- tabulate('G4', 'baroque-lute-13') returns course 1 fret 2 (quality='low_fret')
- tabulate('A1', 'baroque-lute-13') returns course 13 fret 0 (quality='diapason') — only if diapason tuning matches
- tabulate('E4', 'baroque-guitar-5') accounts for re-entrant courses
- Returns empty array for pitches outside instrument range

**Test:**
- Verify F4 on baroque lute → course 1 open
- Verify D4 on baroque lute → course 2 open
- Verify G2 on baroque lute → course 7 open (diapason)
- Verify C#4 on baroque lute → returns positions on courses 1-2 at higher frets
- Verify out-of-range pitch → empty array
- Verify re-entrant handling on baroque guitar course 4

title: voicings tool — chord voicing enumeration
description:
Implement the voicings AgentTool<T> that enumerates all playable voicings for a chord on a given instrument.

**What:**
- Input: { notes: string[], instrument: string, max_stretch?: number }
- For each note in the chord, get all valid positions via tabulate logic
- Enumerate all combinations that satisfy max_stretch constraint
- Rank by: stretch (smaller better), campanella_score (more ringing courses better), open_strings (more open better)
- Return top N voicings (default top 5 to LLM)

**Algorithm:**
1. For each pitch, get positions array
2. Generate all combinations (cartesian product)
3. Filter: no two notes on same course
4. Filter: max fret span ≤ max_stretch (default 4 for lute, 5 for guitar)
5. Score each: stretch (fret span), campanella (adjacent courses ring = good), open strings count
6. Sort by composite score, return top results

**Considerations:**
- Cartesian product can be large — prune early by course conflicts
- max_stretch defaults: 4 for lute (smaller neck), 5 for guitar
- Campanella: notes on adjacent courses that ring simultaneously score higher (idiomatic for baroque)
- For strummed baroque guitar chords: all courses must be included (rasgueado)

**Returns to LLM (content):** Top 5 voicings with stretch and quality descriptions
**Returns to UI (details):** { voicings: Voicing[] } for potential fretboard rendering

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- voicings(['D4', 'F4', 'A3'], 'baroque-lute-13') returns valid voicings
- No voicing has two notes on the same course
- All voicings satisfy max_stretch constraint
- Voicings are ranked (best first)
- Returns empty array for impossible chords

**Test:**
- D minor triad on baroque lute → verify at least one voicing returned
- Verify no same-course conflicts in any result
- Verify all stretches ≤ max_stretch
- Impossible chord (all notes outside range) → empty array

title: check_playability tool
description:
Implement the check_playability AgentTool<T> that validates a passage against instrument constraints.

**What:**
- Input: { bars: Bar[], instrument: string } where Bar has notes with course/fret positions
- Checks:
  1. Stretch violations: fret span in any simultaneous group > max (4 lute, 5 guitar)
  2. Same-course conflicts: two notes on the same course at the same time
  3. Out-of-range: notes beyond instrument fret count or below diapason range
  4. Right-hand pattern feasibility: basic check (> 4 simultaneous notes = impossible)
- Returns difficulty rating: beginner/intermediate/advanced based on weighted scoring

**Difficulty algorithm (from SPEC.md):**
- Count violations (any → at least 'intermediate')
- Max fret stretch per chord (>4 lute / >5 guitar = 'advanced')
- Position shifts per bar (>2 = adds difficulty)
- Simultaneous voice count (>3 sustained = 'advanced' on lute)
- Simple weighted sum → beginner / intermediate / advanced

**Returns to LLM (content):** Violation list + difficulty rating
**Returns to UI (details):** { violations: Violation[], difficulty: string, flagged_bars: number[] }

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Detects stretch violations (fret span too wide)
- Detects same-course conflicts
- Detects out-of-range notes
- Returns difficulty rating
- Clean passage → no violations, appropriate difficulty

**Test:**
- Passage with fret 1 and fret 7 simultaneous → stretch violation
- Passage with two notes on course 1 → same-course conflict
- Passage with fret 10 (beyond max frets) → out-of-range
- Simple scale → beginner difficulty, no violations

title: transpose tool
description:
Implement the transpose AgentTool<T> for interval transposition with range validation.

**What:**
- Input: { source: string, interval: string, instrument: string }
- Transpose all pitches by the given interval
- Check each transposed pitch against instrument range
- Flag out-of-range notes
- Suggest idiomatic keys based on instrument profile

**Idiomatic keys table (from SPEC.md):**
- Baroque lute: D minor, A minor, F major, G minor, C major
- Baroque guitar: A minor, E minor, C major, G major, D minor
- Renaissance lute: G major, D minor, C major, A minor
- Theorbo: D minor, G minor, A minor
- Classical guitar: E minor, A minor, D major, G major, C major

**Algorithm:**
1. Parse each pitch to MIDI number
2. Add interval (in semitones)
3. Convert back to pitch name
4. Check against instrument range (lowest-highest)
5. Suggest nearest idiomatic key if many notes out of range

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Transpose C4 up a P5 → G4
- Transpose a passage from C major to A minor → all pitches shifted
- Out-of-range pitches flagged
- Key suggestions match idiomatic keys table

**Test:**
- Transpose C4 up M3 → E4
- Transpose a D minor passage on baroque lute up P4 → G minor
- Transpose outside range → out_of_range flags populated
- Key suggestions for baroque lute include D minor, A minor

title: diapasons tool
description:
Implement the diapasons AgentTool<T> that returns bass string tuning for a key.

**What:**
- Input: { key: string, instrument?: string }
- Looks up the diapason tuning scheme from the instrument profile
- Returns tuning for courses 7-13 (baroque lute) or 7-14 (theorbo)
- Default instrument: baroque-lute-13

**Tuning schemes (from SPEC.md):**
- D minor / F major: G-F-Eb-D-C-Bb-A (accord ordinaire)
- A minor / C major: G-F-E-D-C-B-A (natural 3rd/7th)
- G minor / Bb major: G-F-Eb-D-C-Bb-A (same as standard)
- D major: G-F#-E-D-C#-B-A (sharp keys, Weiss)
- E minor: G-F#-E-D-C-B-A (natural with F#)

**Returns to LLM:** Human-readable description + LilyPond additionalBassStrings syntax
**Returns to UI:** { courses: DiapasonCourse[] }

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- diapasons('D minor') returns accord ordinaire tuning
- diapasons('A minor') returns natural tuning
- Unknown key → return closest match with warning
- Returns LilyPond syntax string for copy-paste

**Test:**
- D minor → [G, F, Eb, D, C, Bb, A]
- A minor → [G, F, E, D, C, B, A]
- E minor → F# present (not F natural)

title: fretboard tool — SVG diagram renderer
description:
Implement the fretboard AgentTool<T> that renders SVG fretboard diagrams.

**What:**
- Input: { positions: TabPosition[], instrument: string }
- Generates an SVG showing the fretboard with finger positions marked
- Course lines horizontal, fret lines vertical
- Positions shown as filled circles or letters at course/fret intersections
- Open strings marked at nut
- Adapts to instrument (number of courses, frets shown)

**SVG generation:**
- Pure string template — no SVG library dependency
- Fretboard grid: courses as horizontal lines, frets as vertical lines
- Position markers: circles at course/fret
- Labels: course numbers on left, fret numbers on top
- Dimensions: adapt to instrument (6-string guitar vs 13-course lute)
- Show only relevant fret range (not all 19 guitar frets for a 1st-position chord)

**Returns to LLM (content):** Text description of the diagram
**Returns to UI (details):** { svg: string } rendered via registerToolRenderer('fretboard')

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Produces valid SVG
- Shows correct number of courses/strings for the instrument
- Position markers at correct locations
- Open strings marked
- SVG is viewable in browser

**Test:**
- Generate diagram for D minor chord on baroque lute → verify SVG is valid XML

title: EPIC 3: Instrument Profiles
description:
Define YAML instrument profiles for all 7 supported instruments and implement the profile loading/parsing logic. Profiles encode tuning, constraints, fret count, notation type, and instrument-specific features (diapason schemes, stringing variants). These are consumed by: (1) the system prompt builder, (2) the instrument mechanics tools (tabulate, voicings, etc.), (3) the server API endpoint GET /api/instruments.

Instruments: baroque-lute-13, baroque-guitar-5, renaissance-lute-6, theorbo-14, classical-guitar-6, piano, voice (soprano/alto/tenor/bass).

Profiles are YAML files in instruments/ directory. See SPEC.md §Instrument Profiles for exact schema of each profile.

**Key details:**

title: Profile schema and YAML loader
description:
Create a TypeScript module that loads and validates instrument YAML profiles.

**What:** A function loadProfile(id: string): InstrumentProfile that reads instruments/{id}.yaml, parses YAML, and validates against the InstrumentProfile TypeBox schema from types.ts. Also loadAllProfiles(): InstrumentProfile[] for system prompt injection.

**Why:** Both server (GET /api/instruments) and browser (system prompt builder) need to read profiles. The loader handles YAML parsing and validation in one place.

**How:** Use js-yaml npm package. Parse YAML → validate with TypeBox Value.Check() → return typed object.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- loadProfile('baroque-lute-13') returns a valid InstrumentProfile
- loadAllProfiles() returns all 7+ profiles
- Invalid YAML throws a descriptive error
- Tuning arrays are correctly parsed with pitch and note fields

**Test:**

title: Epic 4: LilyPond Templates & Includes
description:
Create LilyPond .ily instrument include files and .ly template files for all supported notation layouts. These are the boilerplate that the LLM fills in — the templates handle staff structure, tuning declarations, MIDI setup, and page formatting. The agent generates the music content; templates handle everything else.

Templates live in templates/, includes in instruments/. Server serves templates via GET /api/templates/:name. LilyPond compiles templates+includes server-side.

Critical: the French letter tab template (french-tab.ly) is the primary output for baroque lute — the most important template in v1.

title: Instrument .ily include files
description:
Create LilyPond .ily include files for each instrument.

**What:** instruments/*.ily files that define LilyPond variables (tuning, staff setup) for each instrument:
- baroque-lute-13.ily: stringTunings for 6 fretted courses, additionalBassStrings for 7 diapasons, fret-letter-tablature-format, RhythmicStaff setup
- baroque-guitar-5.ily: stringTunings for 5 courses with re-entrant notation, tablature format
- renaissance-lute-6.ily: stringTunings for 6 courses (G tuning)
- theorbo-14.ily: stringTunings for 6 fretted + additionalBassStrings for 8 diapasons
- classical-guitar-6.ily: standard guitar tab setup
- piano.ily: grand staff layout (treble+bass)
- voice.ily: voice staff with lyrics

**Key LilyPond syntax:**
```lilypond
% baroque-lute-13.ily
stringTunings = \stringTuning <f' d' a f d a,>
additionalBassStrings = \stringTuning <g, f, ees, d, c, bes,, a,,>
tablatureFormat = #fret-letter-tablature-format
```

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Each .ily file is valid LilyPond (can be \include'd without errors)
- baroque-lute-13.ily defines all 13 courses correctly
- baroque-guitar-5.ily handles re-entrant tuning notation
- All .ily files are self-contained (no external dependencies beyond LilyPond builtins)

**Test:**
- For each .ily file, create a minimal .ly that includes it and compiles:
  `\version "2.24.0" \include "baroque-lute-13.ily" { a4 }`
- Verify compilation succeeds with `lilypond --loglevel=ERROR`

title: French letter tab template
description:
Create templates/french-tab.ly — the primary LilyPond template for baroque lute French letter tablature.

**What:** A complete template implementing the three-staff layout from SPEC.md:
1. RhythmicStaff (rhythm flags above tab, no staff lines)
2. TabStaff (French letter notation with diapasons)
3. Hidden Staff (for correct MIDI output)

**Template structure (from SPEC.md):**
```lilypond
\version "2.24.0"
\include "baroque-lute-13.ily"

rhythm = \relative { ... }  % placeholder for rhythm flags
music = \relative { ... }   % placeholder for music content

\score {
  <<
    \new RhythmicStaff \with {
      \override StaffSymbol.line-count = 0
      \autoBeamOff
      \remove Bar_engraver
    } \rhythm

    \new TabStaff \with {
      tablatureFormat = #fret-letter-tablature-format
      stringTunings = \stringTuning <f' d' a f d a,>
      additionalBassStrings = \stringTuning <g, f, ees, d, c, bes,, a,,>
    } \music

    \new Staff \with {
      \remove "Staff_symbol_engraver"
      \override NoteHead.transparent = ##t
      \override Rest.transparent = ##t
      \override Stem.transparent = ##t
      \override Dots.transparent = ##t
    } \music
  >>
  \layout { }
  \midi { \tempo 4 = 72 }
}
```

**This is the most critical template.** French letter tab is the native notation for baroque lute (the primary instrument). Getting this right is a v1 blocker.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Template compiles with LilyPond 2.24.x producing SVG + MIDI
- French letters (a, b, c, d...) appear on tab lines (not numbers)
- Diapasons render below the staff with correct notation (/a, //a, etc.)
- RhythmicStaff shows rhythm flags above the tab
- Hidden staff produces MIDI but is not visible in output
- Polyphonic voices (\voiceOne, \voiceTwo) work within the TabStaff

**Test:**
- Compile template with sample music → verify SVG output
- Check SVG contains text elements with letters (a, b, c) not numbers
- Compile with polyphonic input → verify two voice layers render
- Play MIDI output → verify pitches are correct (not affected by tab formatting)

title: Additional templates (solo-tab, voice-and-tab, grand-staff, SATB, continuo)
description:
Create remaining LilyPond templates in templates/ directory.

**Templates to create:**
- **solo-tab.ly** — Simple TabStaff for guitar number tablature. Uses standard number format. Include hidden staff for MIDI.
- **tab-and-staff.ly** — TabStaff + standard notation Staff side by side. Useful for guitar arrangements where both representations help.
- **voice-and-tab.ly** — Voice line (lyrics + melody) above lute/guitar tab. For song arrangements like Dowland ayres. Two staves: vocal Staff + TabStaff.
- **voice-and-piano.ly** — Voice line above piano grand staff. Standard art song layout.
- **grand-staff.ly** — Piano grand staff (treble + bass). Standard PianoStaff.
- **satb.ly** — Four-part choral: 4 voices on 2 staves (SA on treble, TB on bass).
- **continuo.ly** — Figured bass: bass line with figures above, realized keyboard below. Uses \figuremode. v2 template but stub it now.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Each template compiles with LilyPond producing valid SVG
- voice-and-tab.ly renders both a vocal staff and tablature staff
- grand-staff.ly uses PianoStaff with connected bar lines
- satb.ly has 4 independent voices

**Test:**
- Compile each template with minimal sample music → all produce SVG
- Verify voice-and-tab.ly output shows both staff types visually distinct

title: Template compilation test suite
description:
Create an integration test that compiles every template with sample music and verifies valid output.

**What:** A test script (test/templates.test.ts or shell script) that:
1. For each .ly template in templates/:
   a. Fills in minimal sample music (a scale or simple chord progression)
   b. Runs lilypond to compile it
   c. Verifies SVG output is valid (non-empty, contains expected SVG elements)
   d. Verifies MIDI output exists (for templates with \midi block)
2. For french-tab.ly specifically:
   a. Verifies French letters appear in SVG (text elements with 'a', 'b', 'c')
   b. Verifies polyphonic voices render correctly
   c. Tests diapason notation

**Why:** Templates are the foundation of all output. If a template doesn't compile, nothing works. This test catches LilyPond regressions and syntax errors early.

**Test approach:** Real LilyPond compilation — no mocking. This is an integration test that requires LilyPond to be installed (via nix shell).

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- All templates compile without errors
- SVG output is valid XML
- MIDI output is non-empty binary
- French tab letters confirmed in baroque lute output
- Test runs in < 30 seconds (LilyPond is fast for small inputs)

title: Frontend & Agent Integration
description:
Wire everything together: system prompt builder, custom tool renderers, and the main.ts entry point that creates the Agent, registers renderers, and connects the ChatPanel + ArtifactsPanel. This is where all the pieces come together into a working application.

The agent's effectiveness depends on the system prompt — it must inject instrument profiles, workflow instructions, and tool usage guidance. The tool renderers provide visual feedback (SVG tablature inline in chat, fretboard diagrams, playability reports).

title: System prompt builder (prompts.ts)
description:
Implement src/prompts.ts — builds the system prompt from instrument profiles and workflow instructions.

**What:**
- buildSystemPrompt(instruments: InstrumentProfile[]): string
- Renders the system prompt template from SPEC.md §System Prompt Design:
  1. Role description (Vellum, music arrangement specialist)
  2. Tool usage instructions (when to call each tool)
  3. Workflow steps (source verification → harmonic analysis → pitch mapping → voice leading → playability → theory check → idiom → output)
  4. Instrument profiles (formatted YAML, one per instrument)
  5. Source-file-first guidance (warn when working from memory)
  6. Auto-compile instruction (call compile after generating .ly)
  7. Artifacts instruction (update side panel after successful compile)

**Why:** The system prompt is the LLM's instruction manual. Without proper tool guidance, the LLM will try to do everything in its head. Without instrument profiles, it won't know the tunings.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- Prompt includes all workflow steps from SPEC.md
- Prompt includes formatted instrument profiles
- Prompt mentions every tool by name with usage guidance
- Prompt includes source-file-first warning
- Prompt is under 8K tokens (rough estimate — profiles are small)

**Test:**
- Build prompt with all profiles → verify it contains 'baroque-lute-13', 'tabulate', 'compile', 'analyze', 'lint', 'theory'
- Verify workflow steps present in order
- Verify source-file-first warning text present

title: Tool renderers (renderers.ts)
Agent wiring (main.ts)
Epic: Shared Types & Schemas
description: Define all shared TypeBox schemas and TypeScript interfaces used across the Vellum tablature toolkit. This epic covers the creation of a single authoritative types module (src/types.ts) that serves as the single source of truth for data structures shared between browser tools, server endpoints, and tool renderers. TypeBox schemas serve dual purpose: runtime validation on the server side and static type generation for TypeScript. Centralizing types here prevents type drift between client and server code. Encompasses schemas for tab positions, voicings, compile results, playability analysis, lint violations, music analysis, instrument profiles, and all tool parameter schemas.
